prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_02: "We're going to talk today about a couple of experiments related to learning in infants or babies."
SPEAKER_02: "Those areas are memory and imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "Memory and imitation, so you read Chapter 8 of your textbook, right?"
SPEAKER_02: "It deals with these topics."
SPEAKER_02: "So I'll have some questions for you."
SPEAKER_02: "The two studies I want to look at, they've been controversial."
SPEAKER_02: "That's because they show these things happening before or earlier than we used to think."
SPEAKER_02: "So first, let's look at an experiment by Caroline Roeve Collier."
SPEAKER_02: "She published an important study that looked at the cognitive abilities of infants."
SPEAKER_02: "What can you tell me about Roeve Collier's study?"
SPEAKER_01: "Um, she had a baby in a crib and above the baby, there was a toy, a mobile hanging above the crib and a ribbon was tied from the mobile to the baby's leg."
SPEAKER_01: "Um, so when the baby kicked, the mobile moved."
SPEAKER_01: "There's a picture in your book."
SPEAKER_01: "So, um, yeah."
SPEAKER_01: "When they returned the baby to the crib weeks later, the baby kicked its leg when it saw the mobile again."
SPEAKER_01: "Its foot wasn't tied to the mobile, but it was still kicking."
SPEAKER_00: "But Roe v. Collier also went on to say that was proof, evidence, uh, that the baby had remembered something, had a memory."
SPEAKER_00: "The baby had kept that information that kicking moves the mobile."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."
SPEAKER_02: "In those experiments, she typically had a two-and-a-half-month-old infant."
SPEAKER_02: "The baby kicks and gets to see the mobile move."
SPEAKER_02: "So Roevee Collier concludes that very young infants can remember retain information like this."
SPEAKER_02: "So what did critics say about her conclusions?"
SPEAKER_02: "Sarah?"
SPEAKER_00: "That it wasn't true."
SPEAKER_00: "It wasn't real memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Yes."
SPEAKER_02: "Good."
SPEAKER_02: "Here's a distinction one critic raised."
SPEAKER_02: "This researcher, a leading expert in the field, said that the baby kicking after seeing the mobile is an example of implicit memory."
SPEAKER_02: "Implicit memory refers to remembering information, but not being consciously aware of remembering."
SPEAKER_02: "For example, many motor skills."
SPEAKER_02: "Remember those are skills involving movement of the body, that kind of thing?"
SPEAKER_02: "Motor skills are forms of implicit memory, like knowing how to ride a bike."
SPEAKER_02: "So the baby's kicking has to do with implicit memory because it involves perception and motor skills."
SPEAKER_02: "The baby kicks and sees a mobile move."
SPEAKER_02: "Now, explicit memory on the other hand is, well, you tell me."
SPEAKER_02: "What is it?"
SPEAKER_00: "That's where you can remember something."
SPEAKER_00: "Remember it consciously."
SPEAKER_00: "You can remember what happened in the past."
SPEAKER_00: "That's what's usually meant by the word memory."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, real memory."
SPEAKER_02: "OK. And most researchers, they would say that ability in the area of explicit memory doesn't happen until a baby is six months to a year old, later than what Roe v. Collier said."
SPEAKER_02: "So that distinction between two different kinds of memory is important in this debate about memory and infants."
SPEAKER_02: "And then there's another thing, another cognitive ability they tested besides memory."
SPEAKER_02: "They tested imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "That's like when a parent smiles and the baby smiles back, or the parent opens their mouth, then the baby opens its mouth."
SPEAKER_02: "Here the researcher was Andrew Meltzoff."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltzolf has done studies with babies in the first few days after they were born."
SPEAKER_02: "What did he find in his experiments?"
SPEAKER_01: "Yeah, he would do things like stick out his tongue and open his mouth and the baby was able to imitate it."
SPEAKER_01: "He thought imitation was biologically based, I think."
SPEAKER_01: "And that's because, wasn't it because they were imitating something right after they'd seen it for the first time?"
SPEAKER_02: "Yes, that's right."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltzoff showed that in the first three days of life, the babies could do a full imitative response."
SPEAKER_02: "And he thinks it's biologically based because this occurs before they've had the opportunity to observe other people engage in these behaviors, like sticking out the tongue."
SPEAKER_02: "Here again other experts disagree."
SPEAKER_02: "They say that this was just an automatic response on the baby's part."
SPEAKER_02: "Not true imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "And true imitation doesn't happen until much later, they think."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltsoff also tested something called deferred imitation."
SPEAKER_02: "It's an imitation that occurs after a time delay of hours or even days."
SPEAKER_02: "Meltsoff showed that nine-month-old infants could imitate actions that they'd seen performed 24 hours earlier, like pushing a button in a box that produced a beeping sound."
SPEAKER_02: "But some experts, like Piaget, believed that deferred imitation doesn't occur until about 18 months of age."
SPEAKER_02: "So you can see that when some of these things begin, remembering imitation isn't entirely certain yet."
SPEAKER_02: "But still, I do think psychologists will eventually find agreement on these issues."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "So we've spent a lot of time in this class studying ecosystems."
SPEAKER_00: "And now I want to talk about a specific instance of an ecosystem changing over time, changing significantly and unexpectedly."
SPEAKER_00: "I'm talking about the Aleutian Islands off the coast of Alaska."
SPEAKER_00: "The Aleutian Islands up until the 18th century were a grassland ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the grasslands on some of the islands have disappeared and what we have there now is tundra, primarily low scrubby vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "And this started happening in the 18th century."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so why?"
SPEAKER_00: "Jeff?"
SPEAKER_01: "Um, excuse me, Professor, but I thought tundra referred to frozen ground."
SPEAKER_01: "Wouldn't all the islands have that?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, right."
SPEAKER_00: "Tundra often refers to a region where the underground soil is permanently frozen."
SPEAKER_00: "And the primary vegetation is moss, lichen and maybe a few dwarf shrubs."
SPEAKER_00: "But it's the vegetation that classifies a region as tundra, not the temperature of the soil."
SPEAKER_00: "Anyway, in the 18th century, traders introduced foxes onto the islands, and those foxes preyed on the ground nesting seabirds."
SPEAKER_01: "And you're saying that changed the entire ecosystem?"
SPEAKER_00: "Oh yes, and drastically."
SPEAKER_00: "Introducing a single species can gradually transform an entire ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "And a study that was done on the Illusion Island shows one way this can happen."
SPEAKER_00: "So, alright."
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes were introduced, and the most obvious impact was a decrease of the seabird population."
SPEAKER_00: "That's a direct impact, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes preyed on the seabirds and they started to disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there were indirect effects too."
SPEAKER_00: "Changes in the soil and vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "And these effects weren't obvious for a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "They were much harder to detect."
SPEAKER_00: "So, this study."
SPEAKER_00: "A biologist led a team of researchers to the Aleutian Islands, where they noticed significant differences in the islands that had foxes and the islands that didn't, especially in the vegetation."
SPEAKER_00: "It was so thick on the islands without foxes that they could barely get through it."
SPEAKER_00: "But on the islands with foxes, it was only up to their ankles."
SPEAKER_00: "And the biologist had a theory about this."
SPEAKER_00: "Can anyone guess what it might be?"
SPEAKER_01: "So the foxes are eating the seabirds."
SPEAKER_01: "Maybe the seabirds were using up the grass, like for nesting materials or something."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, think about what that would mean."
SPEAKER_01: "Oh, right."
SPEAKER_00: "What the biologist thought was, he thought it might have something to do with the soil nutrients."
SPEAKER_00: "So these researchers, to investigate this theory, they analyzed the soil on these islands."
SPEAKER_00: "They wanted to determine the nutrient content to see if it played some kind of role in transforming the vegetation from grassland to tundra."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, what they found was, I think, very interesting."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, first, the soil on the Aleutian Islands is volcanic soil, nutrient-poor soil that formed from volcanic rock."
SPEAKER_00: "And what the researchers learned was that the islands without foxes were receiving regular doses of fertilizer from the droppings of the seabirds the foxes preyed on."
SPEAKER_00: "These seabirds eat fish, and they were bringing ocean-derived nutrients to the islands in the form of droppings when they came there to nest."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the islands with foxes weren't getting this fertilizer because the birds were disappearing."
SPEAKER_00: "So now we see the indirect effects of the foxes, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "The foxes, because of their impact on the seabirds, deprived the soil of fertilizer which caused the grasslands to disappear and be replaced by tundra."
SPEAKER_00: "Tundra vegetation does well in nutrient poor soil."
SPEAKER_00: "Now that's a pretty huge impact and one that no one predicted."
SPEAKER_00: "So what can we do about it?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we want to bring back the seabirds and we're doing that."
SPEAKER_00: "We're removing the foxes and we've been successful."
SPEAKER_00: "Most of the foxes are off the islands now and the seabirds are starting to come back."
SPEAKER_00: "But seabirds, most seabirds that is, have only one offspring a year."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we've learned that there are now only half the number of breeding seabirds on the islands that had the foxes as there are on the islands that didn't."
SPEAKER_00: "And there were major changes in the vegetation, and the foxes were there for a long time."
SPEAKER_00: "So, does this mean that non-native species should never be introduced into an ecosystem?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, not necessarily."
SPEAKER_00: "Scientists introduce species in many different places around the world and for a number of reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "We'll talk more about that over the course of the semester."
SPEAKER_00: "It does point out the dangers of unforeseen consequences though, and it certainly suggests the need for careful planning and for further studies of the effects in habitats such as these where new species have already been introduced."

prompt_text:

SPEAKER_00: "Okay."
SPEAKER_00: "If I asked about the earliest thing you can remember, I'll bet for most of you your earliest memory would be from about age three, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, that's true for most adults."
SPEAKER_00: "We can't remember anything that happened before the age of three."
SPEAKER_00: "And this phenomenon is so widespread and well documented, it has a name."
SPEAKER_00: "It's called Childhood Amnesia and was first documented in 1893."
SPEAKER_00: "As I said, this phenomenon refers to adults not being able to remember childhood incidents."
SPEAKER_00: "It's not children trying to remember events from last month or last year."
SPEAKER_00: "Of course it follows that if you can't remember an incident as a child, you probably won't remember it as an adult."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay?"
SPEAKER_00: "So why is this?"
SPEAKER_00: "What are the reasons for childhood amnesia?"
SPEAKER_00: "Once a popular explanation was that childhood memories are repressed, the memories are disturbing, so that as adults we keep them buried."
SPEAKER_00: "And so we can't recall them."
SPEAKER_00: "And this is based on, well it's not based on the kind of solid research and lab testing I want to talk about today."
SPEAKER_00: "So let's put that explanation aside and concentrate on just two, okay?"
SPEAKER_00: "It could be that as children we do form memories of things prior to age three, but forget them as we grow older."
SPEAKER_00: "That's one explanation."
SPEAKER_00: "Another possibility is that children younger than three lack some cognitive capacity for memory."
SPEAKER_00: "And that idea that children are unable to form memories, that's been the dominant belief in psychology for the past 100 years."
SPEAKER_00: "And this idea is very much tied to two things, the theories of Jean Piaget and also to language development in children."
SPEAKER_00: "So, Piaget's theory of cognitive development."
SPEAKER_00: "Piaget suggested that because they don't have language, children younger than 18 to 24 months live in the here and now, that is, they lack the means to symbolically represent objects and events that are not physically present."
SPEAKER_00: "Everybody get that?"
SPEAKER_00: "Piaget proposed that young children don't have a way to represent things that aren't right in front of them."
SPEAKER_00: "That's what language does, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "Words represent things, ideas."
SPEAKER_00: "Once language starts to develop from about age two, they do have a system for symbolic representation and can talk about things which aren't in their immediate environment, including the past."
SPEAKER_00: "Of course, he didn't claim that infants don't have any sort of memory."
SPEAKER_00: "It's acknowledged that they can recognize some stimuli, like faces,"
SPEAKER_00: "And for many years this model was very much in favor in psychology even though memory tests were never performed on young children."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, finally in the 1980s a study was done and this study showed that very young children under the age of two do have the capacity for recall."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, if the children can't talk, how was recall tested?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, that's a good question, since the capacity for recall has always been linked with the ability to talk."
SPEAKER_00: "So the researchers set up an experiment using imitation-based tasks, adults use props, toys or other objects to demonstrate an action that had two steps."
SPEAKER_00: "The children were asked to imitate the steps immediately, and then again after delays of one or more months."
SPEAKER_00: "And even after a delay, the children could recall or replicate the action, the objects used, the steps involved, and the order of the steps."
SPEAKER_00: "Even children as young as nine months."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, tests showed that there was a faster way to forgetting among the youngest children."
SPEAKER_00: "But most importantly, it showed that the development of recall did not depend on language development."
SPEAKER_00: "And that was an important finding."
SPEAKER_00: "I guess I should add that the findings don't say that there was no connection, no connection between the development of language and memory."
SPEAKER_00: "There's some evidence that being able to talk about an event does lead to having a stronger memory of that event."
SPEAKER_00: "But that doesn't seem to be the real issue here."
SPEAKER_00: "So back to our question about the cause of childhood amnesia."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there is something called the rate of forgetting."
SPEAKER_00: "And childhood amnesia may reflect a high rate of forgetting."
SPEAKER_00: "In other words, children under the age of three do form memories and do so without language."
SPEAKER_00: "But they forget the memories at a fast rate, probably faster than adults do."
SPEAKER_00: "Researchers have set a standard, sort of an expected rate of forgetting, but that expected rate was set based on tests done on adults."
SPEAKER_00: "So what is the rate of forgetting for children under the age of three?"
SPEAKER_00: "We expect it to be high, but the tests to prove this really haven't been done yet."

SPEAKER_02: "The original definition of symbiosis is pretty simple."

SPEAKER_02: "So any close relationship between two organisms of different species would be considered a symbiotic relationship, including positive and negative relationships."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "Okay, I know you all have a lot of questions about this lab assignment that's coming up, so I'm going to take a little time this morning to discuss it."
SPEAKER_00: "So, you know the assignment has to do with spectroscopy, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "And your reading should help you get a good idea of what that's all about."
SPEAKER_00: "But let's talk about spectroscopy a little now, just to cover the basics."
SPEAKER_00: "What is spectroscopy?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, the simplest definition I can give you is that"
SPEAKER_00: "Spectroscopy is the study of the interaction between matter and light."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, visible light consists of different colors or wavelengths, which together make up what's called a spectrum, a band of colors like you see in a rainbow."
SPEAKER_00: "And all substances, all forms of matter, can be distinguished according to what wavelengths of light they absorb and which ones they reflect."
SPEAKER_00: "It's like, well, every element has what we call its own spectral signature."
SPEAKER_00: "If we can read that signature, we can identify the element."
SPEAKER_00: "And that's exactly what spectroscopy does."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, laser spectroscopy, which is the focus of your assignment, works by measuring very precisely what parts of the spectrum are absorbed by different substances."
SPEAKER_00: "And it has applications in a lot of different disciplines."
SPEAKER_00: "And your assignment will be to choose a discipline that interests you and devise an experiment."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, I'm going to talk about art."
SPEAKER_00: "I'm interested in art."
SPEAKER_00: "And to me, it's interesting how spectroscopy is used to analyze art."
SPEAKER_00: "Let's say a museum curator comes to you with a problem."
SPEAKER_00: "She's come across this painting that appears to be an original, let's say a Rembrandt, and she wants to acquire it for her museum."
SPEAKER_00: "But she's got a problem."
SPEAKER_00: "She's not absolutely certain it's an original."
SPEAKER_00: "So what do you do?"
SPEAKER_00: "How do you determine whether the painting is authentic?"
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, think about the scientific process."
SPEAKER_00: "You've got a question."
SPEAKER_00: "Is the painting a Rembrandt?"
SPEAKER_00: "So first, you'd need to make a list of characteristics the painting would have to have to be a Rembrandt."
SPEAKER_00: "Then you have to discover whether the painting in question has those characteristics."
SPEAKER_00: "So first of all, you'll need to know the techniques Rembrandt used when he applied paint to Canvas."
SPEAKER_00: "His brushstrokes, how thickly he applied his paint,"
SPEAKER_00: "So you'd need to work with an art historian who has expert knowledge of Rembrandt's style."
SPEAKER_00: "You'd have to know when he created his paintings, what pigments he used, in other words, what ingredients he used to make different colors of paint, because the ingredients used in paints and binding agents, plus barnishes, finishes, what have you, have changed over time."
SPEAKER_00: "Since you're trying to verify that it's a Rembrandt,"
SPEAKER_00: "The ingredients in the pigment would need to have been used during Rembrandt's lifetime in the 17th century."
SPEAKER_00: "And that's where chemistry comes in."
SPEAKER_00: "You've got to find out what's in those pigments, learn their composition, and that requires lab work, detective work, really, in a word, spectroscopy."
SPEAKER_00: "So how do we use spectroscopy?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, we put an infrared microscope, a spectroscope,"
SPEAKER_00: "On tiny, tiny bits of paint and using ultraviolet light, we can see the spectral signature of each component part of the pigment."
SPEAKER_00: "Then we compare these signatures with those of particular elements like zinc or lead to determine what the pigment was made of."
SPEAKER_00: "So you can see why this type of analysis requires a knowledge of the history of pigments, right?"
SPEAKER_00: "How and when they were made."
SPEAKER_00: "Say we determine a pigment was made with zinc, for example."
SPEAKER_00: "We know the spectral signature of zinc."
SPEAKER_00: "and it matches that of the paint sample."
SPEAKER_00: "We also know that Zink wasn't discovered until the 18th century, and since Rembrandt lived during the 17th century, we know he couldn't have painted it."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, spectroscopy has a very distinct advantage over previous methods of analyzing artworks because it's not invasive."
SPEAKER_00: "You don't have to remove big chips of paint to do your analysis, which is what other methods require."
SPEAKER_00: "All you do is train the microscope on tiny flecks of paint and analyze them."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, a word or two about restoration."
SPEAKER_00: "Sometimes original artworks appear questionable or inauthentic because they've had so many restorers add touch-up layers to cover up damage, damage from the paint having deteriorated over time."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, spectroscopy can reveal the composition of those touch-up layers too, so we can find out when they were applied."
SPEAKER_00: "Then, if we want to undo some bad restoration attempts, we can determine what kind of process we can use to remove them, to dissolve the paint and uncover the original."

prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "About 30 years ago, a geologist named Edward Cotter that's C-O-T-T-E-R published a paper that contained a very interesting hypothesis."
SPEAKER_00: "He was studying ancient rivers in the North American mountain chain."
SPEAKER_00: "And he noticed that about 450 million years ago, rivers started to behave differently."
SPEAKER_00: "Before then, rivers were wide, shallow and straight."
SPEAKER_00: "But after that time, they became deeper and had more curves."
SPEAKER_00: "They became increasingly meandering."
SPEAKER_00: "And that's actually how rivers behave to this day."
SPEAKER_00: "So, why might this change have happened?"
SPEAKER_01: "Maybe there was some kind of climate shift?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, lots of climate shifts have happened since then."
SPEAKER_01: "Was the change worldwide or just in that geographical area?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, Cotter speculated that rivers changed worldwide, but he couldn't prove it because he only had evidence from the one North American mountain chain."
SPEAKER_00: "But his studies gave him an idea about why rivers started to change."
SPEAKER_00: "He hypothesized it had to do with the spread of plant life on Earth."
SPEAKER_01: "So there was no plant life before 450 million years ago?"
SPEAKER_00: "Very little according to fossil records."
SPEAKER_00: "Anyway, geologists were intrigued by this hypothesis, which claims that as plants evolved and spread, they had an effect on the terrain and rivers."
SPEAKER_00: "In the past 30 years, more studies have been done, and now we have a lot of data about river systems from around 450 million years ago from all over the world."
SPEAKER_00: "In a recent study, a couple of researchers gathered together the existing data and combined them with their own new field data to get a comprehensive picture of the situation."
SPEAKER_00: "Their study was specifically designed to identify changes in the shapes of rivers during the time period when vegetation was evolving."
SPEAKER_00: "And when the researchers compared the data about river shapes with data they had collected about plant life from the same period, the data seemed to prove Cotter's hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, but how did plant life affect rivers?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, in order to answer that question, we need to look at the geological evidence."
SPEAKER_00: "You see, as rivers flow, they leave layers of sediment behind that eventually fossilize."
SPEAKER_00: "The content, thickness, and shape of these fossilized layers and rocks give us information about how rivers flowed."
SPEAKER_00: "The earliest records from 500 million years ago show that the sediment in river deposits was largely composed of coarse grains of sand and gravel."
SPEAKER_00: "That tells us that rivers weren't defined."
SPEAKER_00: "They were very shallow and wide, almost like floods."
SPEAKER_00: "But around the time of the rise of plant life, the content of those sediment layers began to change."
SPEAKER_00: "The coarse grains became much finer, and we see evidence of mud."
SPEAKER_00: "This suggests that plants promoted the preservation of mud when they sent their roots into the ground."
SPEAKER_00: "The roots help to reinforce the ground, which in turn allowed for the creation of riverbanks."
SPEAKER_00: "And we also see evidence of a process called lateral accretion."
SPEAKER_00: "Lateral accretion happens when water flows around a curve, a bend, in a riverbed."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the speed of the flow on the outside of the bend is fastest and slowest on the inside of the bend."
SPEAKER_00: "This sets up what's called a secondary flow across the river bottom."
SPEAKER_00: "The fast-flowing water on the outside of the bend digs out material from the riverbank and pushes this material laterally across the bottom, and it gets deposited on the other side of the river, on the inner side of the bend."
SPEAKER_00: "So when we see in the sediment layers evidence of lateral accretion erosion on one side and deposits on the other, that's an indicator that a meandering river existed."
SPEAKER_00: "And according to the study, strong evidence of lateral accretion appears in the geological record."
SPEAKER_00: "At the same time, there's also evidence of plants with underground root systems."
SPEAKER_00: "This suggests that plants promoted the development of modern rivers by creating stable banks, which resulted in the flow of water in single meandering channels."
SPEAKER_01: "So it looks like the researchers were able to prove the hypothesis."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there's no denying that the study presents a very strong case, but some questions about this hypothesis remain."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, it's well known that on other planets, like Mars, there's clear evidence of meandering rivers."
SPEAKER_00: "But is there evidence of vegetation on Mars?"
SPEAKER_00: "I think not."

SPEAKER_01: "They weighed up to 100 tons, 20 times as much as elephants."

SPEAKER_01: "There's evidence of sauropods in the fossil record for an unusually long time, over 100 million years."
SPEAKER_01: "So, why were sauropods so successful?"

SPEAKER_01: "Large animals like elephants say they require much more food and energy and have fewer offspring than smaller animals."
SPEAKER_01: "This makes maintaining a population harder."
SPEAKER_01: "The largest animals today don't live on land, but in the ocean where food's easier to find."
SPEAKER_01: "A blue whale, for instance, can eat up to 8,000 pounds of food a day, and they give birth only once every few years."
SPEAKER_01: "We also know that body heat, that, well, large animals can't easily get rid of excess body heat."
SPEAKER_01: "But for an ocean-going whale, that's not a problem."
SPEAKER_01: "For 100-ton land animal, it can be."
SPEAKER_01: "For years we've assumed it was the abundant plant life of the Mesozoic that allowed these giants to thrive."
SPEAKER_01: "However, we now know that since oxygen levels were much lower in the Mesozoic than we'd assumed, there was much less plant life for sauropods to eat than we'd thought."
SPEAKER_01: "So now, we're trying to understand the biology of sauropods, comparing their fossils to the anatomy of modern animals to get a better idea of how they lived."
SPEAKER_01: "What we found is that sauropods were experts at conserving energy."
SPEAKER_01: "They had enormous stomach capacity, the ability to digest food over a long period, converting it to energy at a slower pace, saving it for later."
SPEAKER_01: "For animals with small stomachs, it takes lots of energy to constantly look for food and then digest it."
SPEAKER_01: "With larger stomachs and slower digestion, you don't need as much energy."
SPEAKER_00: "Does scientists actually know about sauropods from looking at some ancient bones compared to all we can learn from modern animals?"
SPEAKER_00: "And comparisons between animals that lived millions of years apart?"
SPEAKER_01: "more like guessing."
SPEAKER_01: "For example, let's look at one of these comparisons."
SPEAKER_01: "We know sauropods couldn't chew food."
SPEAKER_01: "Their skulls show they had no chewing muscles."
SPEAKER_01: "Lots of modern animals like birds and reptiles also can't chew food."
SPEAKER_01: "They need to swallow it whole."
SPEAKER_01: "But modern animals have an interesting aid for digesting food."
SPEAKER_01: "They swallow stones, stones that are used to help grind up the food before it's actually digested in the stomach."
SPEAKER_01: "These stones are called gastroliths."
SPEAKER_01: "Gastroliths make food easier to digest, essentially smashing food up, just as we do when we chew."
SPEAKER_01: "Over time, gastroliths inside the animal are ground down and become smooth and rounded."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, sauropod fossils are commonly found with smoothed stones."
SPEAKER_01: "For years we thought these were gastroliths."

SPEAKER_01: "A recent study measured the gastrolis in modern animals in ostriches."
SPEAKER_01: "And the study showed that ostriches need to ingest about 1% of their total body weight in gastrolis."
SPEAKER_01: "But we've been able to determine that the stones found with sauropods total much less proportionally."
SPEAKER_01: "Less than a tenth of 1% of their body weight."
SPEAKER_01: "So now we're not quite sure what these sauropod stones were used for."
SPEAKER_01: "It could be they were accidentally ingested as the sauropods foraged for food, that they served no real purpose."
SPEAKER_01: "Other researchers speculate that sauropods ingested these stones as a source of some of the minerals they needed, such as calcium."
SPEAKER_01: "So, the original hypothesis, that the stones found with sauropods were gastroliths, even though it hasn't been supported, has helped us to make new hypotheses, which may eventually lead to the answer."

SPEAKER_01: "Okay, back in the 1930s, a biologist named G.F. Gauza first proposed what's known as Gauza's hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "Gauss has said that whenever you've got two similar species competing for the exact same limited resources, one of them will have some sort of advantage, however slight, that'll eventually enable this species to dominate and ultimately exclude the other one, even cause it to become extinct."
SPEAKER_01: "That's why Gauss' hypothesis came to be called the competitive exclusion principle."
SPEAKER_01: "Gauza did some lab experiments, like placing two paramecium species in the same environment where they'd have to compete for the same food."
SPEAKER_01: "He found that over time, one species was consistently able to drive out the other, to eliminate it from the habitat, just as his hypothesis predicted."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, one of the early criticisms of Gauss' hypothesis was that, sure, it works in simple lab experiments where you have just two competing species in a controlled environment, but the hypothesis falls apart when applied to natural ecosystems where things are more complex."
SPEAKER_01: "Now, it's true that in the real world there are lots of examples that seem to contradict the hypothesis."
SPEAKER_01: "For example, in the forests of New England, in the northeastern United States, there are some small songbirds called warblers."
SPEAKER_01: "And right in the same area, you've got five species of warbler, all about the same size and all having similar diets of insects, insects that are found on and around trees."
SPEAKER_01: "Yet these five warbler species all managed to coexist."
SPEAKER_01: "There's no dominance, no exclusion of one species by another."
SPEAKER_01: "How's this possible?"
SPEAKER_01: "Well, turns out that one warbler species feeds in the uppermost branches, while others favor the middle branches and others feed toward the bottom of the tree."
SPEAKER_01: "Also, each warbler species breeds at a different time of year."
SPEAKER_01: "This way, the period of peak food requirement when the birds are feeding their chicks varies from one species to the next."
SPEAKER_00: "Yes, Mark."
SPEAKER_00: "But does that really contradict Gauss' hypothesis?"
SPEAKER_00: "Because, I mean, are those different warbler species really competing for the same food?"
SPEAKER_00: "I don't think so."
SPEAKER_00: "I think they're more like, you know, almost cooperating so they don't have to compete."
SPEAKER_00: "Excellent."
SPEAKER_01: "To the casual observer, the warblers do seem to contradict Gauss' hypothesis, since they all live in the same place and eat the same types of insects."
SPEAKER_01: "But if you observe these birds more closely, the warbler species are not really competing with one another for the exact same food at the exact same time."
SPEAKER_01: "Which brings us to a really important concept in ecology."
SPEAKER_01: "The niche."
SPEAKER_01: "Mark, can you tell us what an ecological niche is?"
SPEAKER_00: "The place where the plant or animal lives."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, it's habitat."
SPEAKER_00: "For example?"
SPEAKER_00: "Like the polar bear living in the Arctic on the ice sheet."
SPEAKER_00: "The Arctic is its niche, the habitat it's adapted to survive in."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, that's what most people think of."
SPEAKER_01: "But for biologists, the concept of a niche also includes the way an organism functions in its habitat, how it interacts with other plant and animal species, with the soil, the air, the water, and so on."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, now let's put it all together."
SPEAKER_01: "If you have two similar species competing in the same niche, what's going to happen?"
SPEAKER_01: "Susan?"
SPEAKER_01: "One will dominate the other and eventually eliminate it."
SPEAKER_01: "Okay, so what could the weaker species do to improve its chances of survival?"
SPEAKER_01: "Maybe just move to some other area, you know, away from the competitor?"
SPEAKER_01: "That's one possibility."
SPEAKER_01: "But think of the scientific definition of a niche."
SPEAKER_01: "Think about the warblers."
SPEAKER_00: "Mark?"
SPEAKER_00: "Maybe it could find some new way of functioning in its habitat, so it wouldn't have to compete with the dominant species."
SPEAKER_00: "Keep the same habitat, but not the exact same niche."
SPEAKER_01: "Yes, um, and there are many ways to do that."
SPEAKER_01: "The dominant species feeds in one part of the tree, and you feed in another."
SPEAKER_00: "If the dominant species needs lots of water, you develop the ability to survive on very little water."
SPEAKER_01: "You survive on what's left over."
SPEAKER_01: "Water, food, nesting, or breeding sites, whatever."

Transition sentence: To figure this out, Borja is raising some male bowerbirds in captivity, some with older birds to learn from, and some without.
prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "Biologists study complex animal behaviors to find out why these behaviors evolved, how they function, how they're transferred to the next generation, and so forth."
SPEAKER_00: "The courting behavior of the bower bird is studied for all these reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "A bower bird is about the size of a pigeon and found mostly in Australia's rainforest, which is a small but ecologically diverse area in northeastern Australia."
SPEAKER_00: "Bower refers to the male bird's nest or structure."
SPEAKER_00: "Now most birds, including female bower birds, build nests to raise chicks, but male bower birds build elaborate structures to help them attract mates."
SPEAKER_00: "Take a look at this bower bird decorating the outside of his structure."
SPEAKER_00: "As you see, construction takes place on the forest floor, not in a tree."
SPEAKER_00: "And apparently the female bower birds like blue decorations."
SPEAKER_00: "The male decorates the bower with feathers, flowers, pebbles, and shells."
SPEAKER_00: "We've found bowers decorated with bright blue clothes pins probably snatched from someone's backyard."
SPEAKER_00: "Bower birds are also known to steal ornaments from other bower birds."
SPEAKER_00: "When the bower is ready, a female bird may come over to it and watch the male perform for her."
SPEAKER_00: "He'll dance, sing, or rather chirp, often quite energetically."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so there are 17 different kinds of bower birds, all classified by the type of bower they build."
SPEAKER_00: "And there are three major types of bower."
SPEAKER_00: "First is the matte bower."
SPEAKER_00: "Matte bowers are the least elaborate and the easiest to build."
SPEAKER_00: "They're basically thick pads made from sticks and leaves surrounded by ornaments."
SPEAKER_00: "With matbowers, there's no real shelter for the females, so when the males are doing their performance, they keep their bellies close to the ground, signaling they will not attempt to mate unless invited to do so."
SPEAKER_00: "In this way, they don't frighten or overwhelm the female."
SPEAKER_00: "Next is the maypole bower."
SPEAKER_00: "Maypole bowers are built around a small tree, a sapling."
SPEAKER_00: "They sometimes have two towers of twigs with a little covered bridge between them."
SPEAKER_00: "Maypole bowers often have a moss lawn, which is kept neat and fresh."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the most elaborate bower is the avenue bower, like the one you saw in the slide."
SPEAKER_00: "First, the bird constructs an avenue which is like a tent out of twigs and then it places ornaments all around it."
SPEAKER_00: "The female can actually go into this bower to watch the male's performance."
SPEAKER_00: "And we believe that bowers generally, and especially avenue-bowers, provide protection for the female."
SPEAKER_00: "Protection against unwanted mating, perhaps."
SPEAKER_00: "Or so she won't feel threatened during the courting dance."
SPEAKER_00: "Oh, oh, and the male may even paint the inside walls of his bower."
SPEAKER_00: "He paints with saliva, saliva mixed with chewed up blueberries, along with charcoal."
SPEAKER_00: "So let's consider the factors that drive male bower birds to create these structures."
SPEAKER_00: "One biologist, Gerald Borgia, has been studying bower birds for more than 20 years."
SPEAKER_00: "Borgia found, well, first recall that many bird species select their mates based on the attractiveness of their feathers or plumage."
SPEAKER_00: "The peacock's a prime example."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, one biologist hypothesized that the bower birds that built the most elaborate bowers had the least colorful plumage."
SPEAKER_00: "But Borja has disproved that hypothesis."
SPEAKER_00: "He found no significant relationship between dull feathers and fancy bowers."
SPEAKER_00: "Borja has found differences in the bowers based on where they're located."
SPEAKER_00: "The fanciest ones tend to be in valleys, as opposed to hilltops."
SPEAKER_00: "He thinks this has something to do with the available light."
SPEAKER_00: "Hilltops are often cloud covered so females can't see the bowers very well."
SPEAKER_00: "In the valleys, though, there's more sunshine."
SPEAKER_00: "In another study, they found that 75% of females return to the same mate year after year and visit no other bower."
SPEAKER_00: "So you have to wonder why the males would go to such trouble every year, you know?"
SPEAKER_00: "Especially since there's no guarantee, especially for young males, that any female will choose them."
SPEAKER_00: "Who knows?"
SPEAKER_00: "Maybe one of you will shed some light on this one day."
SPEAKER_00: "Borja's also very interested in finding out whether bowerbird behaviors are learned from their elders, or whether these behaviors are encoded in their genes at birth."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, like painting."
SPEAKER_00: "Do they learn to paint by watching another male bird, or do they just know how to do it?"
SPEAKER_00: "To figure this out, Borja is raising some male bowerbirds in captivity, some with older birds to learn from, and some without."

SPEAKER_00: "Biologists study complex animal behaviors to find out why these behaviors evolved, how they function, how they're transferred to the next generation, and so forth."
SPEAKER_00: "The courting behavior of the bower bird is studied for all these reasons."
SPEAKER_00: "A bower bird is about the size of a pigeon and found mostly in Australia's rainforest, which is a small but ecologically diverse area in northeastern Australia."
SPEAKER_00: "Bower refers to the male bird's nest or structure."
SPEAKER_00: "Now most birds, including female bower birds, build nests to raise chicks, but male bower birds build elaborate structures to help them attract mates."
SPEAKER_00: "Take a look at this bower bird decorating the outside of his structure."
SPEAKER_00: "As you see, construction takes place on the forest floor, not in a tree."
SPEAKER_00: "And apparently the female bower birds like blue decorations."
SPEAKER_00: "The male decorates the bower with feathers, flowers, pebbles, and shells."
SPEAKER_00: "We've found bowers decorated with bright blue clothes pins probably snatched from someone's backyard."
SPEAKER_00: "Bower birds are also known to steal ornaments from other bower birds."
SPEAKER_00: "When the bower is ready, a female bird may come over to it and watch the male perform for her."
SPEAKER_00: "He'll dance, sing, or rather chirp, often quite energetically."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, so there are 17 different kinds of bower birds, all classified by the type of bower they build."
SPEAKER_00: "And there are three major types of bower."
SPEAKER_00: "First is the matte bower."
SPEAKER_00: "Matte bowers are the least elaborate and the easiest to build."
SPEAKER_00: "They're basically thick pads made from sticks and leaves surrounded by ornaments."
SPEAKER_00: "With matbowers, there's no real shelter for the females, so when the males are doing their performance, they keep their bellies close to the ground, signaling they will not attempt to mate unless invited to do so."
SPEAKER_00: "In this way, they don't frighten or overwhelm the female."
SPEAKER_00: "Next is the maypole bower."
SPEAKER_00: "Maypole bowers are built around a small tree, a sapling."
SPEAKER_00: "They sometimes have two towers of twigs with a little covered bridge between them."
SPEAKER_00: "Maypole bowers often have a moss lawn, which is kept neat and fresh."
SPEAKER_00: "Now the most elaborate bower is the avenue bower, like the one you saw in the slide."
SPEAKER_00: "First, the bird constructs an avenue which is like a tent out of twigs and then it places ornaments all around it."
SPEAKER_00: "The female can actually go into this bower to watch the male's performance."
SPEAKER_00: "And we believe that bowers generally, and especially avenue-bowers, provide protection for the female."
SPEAKER_00: "Protection against unwanted mating, perhaps."
SPEAKER_00: "Or so she won't feel threatened during the courting dance."
SPEAKER_00: "Oh, oh, and the male may even paint the inside walls of his bower."
SPEAKER_00: "He paints with saliva, saliva mixed with chewed up blueberries, along with charcoal."
SPEAKER_00: "So let's consider the factors that drive male bower birds to create these structures."
SPEAKER_00: "One biologist, Gerald Borgia, has been studying bower birds for more than 20 years."
SPEAKER_00: "Borgia found, well, first recall that many bird species select their mates based on the attractiveness of their feathers or plumage."
SPEAKER_00: "The peacock's a prime example."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, one biologist hypothesized that the bower birds that built the most elaborate bowers had the least colorful plumage."
SPEAKER_00: "But Borja has disproved that hypothesis."
SPEAKER_00: "He found no significant relationship between dull feathers and fancy bowers."
SPEAKER_00: "Borja has found differences in the bowers based on where they're located."
SPEAKER_00: "The fanciest ones tend to be in valleys, as opposed to hilltops."
SPEAKER_00: "He thinks this has something to do with the available light."
SPEAKER_00: "Hilltops are often cloud covered so females can't see the bowers very well."
SPEAKER_00: "In the valleys, though, there's more sunshine."
SPEAKER_00: "In another study, they found that 75% of females return to the same mate year after year and visit no other bower."
SPEAKER_00: "So you have to wonder why the males would go to such trouble every year, you know?"
SPEAKER_00: "Especially since there's no guarantee, especially for young males, that any female will choose them."
SPEAKER_00: "Who knows?"
SPEAKER_00: "Maybe one of you will shed some light on this one day."
SPEAKER_00: "Borja's also very interested in finding out whether bowerbird behaviors are learned from their elders, or whether these behaviors are encoded in their genes at birth."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, like painting."
SPEAKER_00: "Do they learn to paint by watching another male bird, or do they just know how to do it?"
SPEAKER_00: "To figure this out, Borja is raising some male bowerbirds in captivity, some with older birds to learn from, and some without."

SPEAKER_00: "Meanwhile though, the planets farther out from the sun, their gravitational force kept on pulling in gas molecules, and these colder planets could accrete and hold onto huge amounts of gas."
SPEAKER_00: "And you ended up with giant planets, such as Jupiter, composed largely of gas."
SPEAKER_00: "So the final result of this accretion is what you see in our solar system."
SPEAKER_00: "Rocky planets nearer the sun and gas giants farther out."
SPEAKER_00: "And then there's the oddball Pluto, which we'll just ignore for the time being."

SPEAKER_00: "And this was not just a great first in itself, but the discovery of 51 Pegasi B, that's what they named this first extrasolar planet,"
SPEAKER_00: "This immediately challenged the core accretion theory of planet formation."
SPEAKER_00: "That's because 51 Pegasi B is a gas giant that orbits very close to its sun."
SPEAKER_00: "In fact, eight times closer to its sun than Mercury, which is the closest planet to our sun."
SPEAKER_00: "Eight times closer."
SPEAKER_00: "And a gas giant, which theory would not have predicted so near."
SPEAKER_00: "Clearly, a different theory of planet formation was needed."
SPEAKER_00: "hence the disk instability theory."
SPEAKER_00: "The disk instability theory had been around for a while, but hadn't gained much attention until the discovery of 51 Pegasi B."
SPEAKER_00: "The theory basically says that if the gaseous disk around the newborn star is massive enough and cold enough"
SPEAKER_00: "then any unevenness of gravity within the disk could cause clumps, even giant clumps of gases to form in as little as a thousand years."
SPEAKER_00: "And those clumps could then become gas giant planets."
SPEAKER_00: "So the disk instability theory says giant planets could form near a sun without needing a long time to do it."
SPEAKER_00: "Funny, isn't it?"
SPEAKER_00: "How one little, okay, one momentous discovery can change what we thought we knew was true."

SPEAKER_00: "And the powerful gravity of such a large planet holds onto its massive atmosphere, even when it's so close to that sun."
SPEAKER_00: "So we find this one gas giant planet orbiting very close to its sun."
SPEAKER_00: "And then, well, it turns out that 51 Pegasi B is not at all unique."
SPEAKER_00: "that it's just the first of a number of large gaseous planets we found that we like to call hot Jupiters, because they're about the size of Jupiter, or larger, and close enough to their suns to be very, very hot."
SPEAKER_00: "So if all we believed about planet formation was based on our own solar system, and it turns out that our solar system lacks at least one fairly common type of planet, then what does that say about what we really know?"

Transition sentence: However, some recent studies suggest that other factors may also play roles in the development of an island's biogeography.
prompt_text:
Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "So we're talking about biogeography."
SPEAKER_00: "Biogeography is the geographic distribution of plants and animals in an environment."
SPEAKER_00: "And as you know, biogeography varies from ecosystem to ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, one type of ecosystem that we haven't discussed yet is the island environment."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, islands have always been interesting to study because their isolation creates a kind of laboratory for examining their ecosystems."
SPEAKER_00: "In the 1960s, Robert MacArthur and E. O. Wilson developed a theory they called the equilibrium theory of island biogeography."
SPEAKER_00: "And their theory states that over time islands achieve equilibrium, a balance in terms of the number of species of plants, animals, insects, etc., that they can support."
SPEAKER_00: "And they, that is MacArthur and Wilson, developed calculations to predict the point at which equilibrium is achieved."
SPEAKER_00: "and the number of species that a given island would be able to sustain."
SPEAKER_00: "According to their theory, the total number of species depends on two things, the size and location of the island."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, they predict that there will be more species on bigger islands and that there will be more species on islands that are closer to the mainland."
SPEAKER_00: "So how does this work?"
SPEAKER_00: "According to MacArthur and Wilson, there are two opposing forces constantly at work in an island environment, pushing the island toward the state of equilibrium."
SPEAKER_00: "The first force is the immigration rate."
SPEAKER_00: "The immigration rate tracks the addition of new species to an island."
SPEAKER_00: "And they argue that this rate declines over time, that there are more new species early on when the island's still empty and there's little competition for space."
SPEAKER_00: "However, as time progresses, there'll be fewer and fewer new species that can compete with established species."
SPEAKER_00: "It's not impossible for a new species to be introduced at this late stage."
SPEAKER_00: "It might adapt better to some change in the evolving habitat, but it is definitely tougher."
SPEAKER_00: "The second force is the extinction rate, and this is the rate at which species disappear from the island."
SPEAKER_00: "In contrast with the immigration rate, this is a force that increases over time."
SPEAKER_00: "In the beginning, when there's plenty of space, species don't need to compete with each other quite so much."
SPEAKER_00: "But as species get settled on the island and the amount of space for each species declines, conditions become much more competitive."
SPEAKER_00: "And it's not just the weakest species that may disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "On a crowded island, there are likely to be smaller numbers of every species, which makes them all more vulnerable to things like sudden changes in the climate or some kind of natural disaster."
SPEAKER_00: "So there are these two opposing forces."
SPEAKER_00: "And at a certain point in the island's ecological evolution, they cancel each other out."
SPEAKER_00: "Immigration matches extinction."
SPEAKER_00: "At that point, equilibrium is achieved."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, if you accept these assumptions, then MacArthur and Wilson's predictions about size and location make sense."
SPEAKER_00: "If an island is bigger, then there's more space for a greater number of species to compete and survive."
SPEAKER_00: "And if one island is closer or easier to get to from the mainland than another,"
SPEAKER_00: "then more species will be able to immigrate to the island from the mainland and establish themselves there before a state of equilibrium occurs."
SPEAKER_00: "Now notice what this theory doesn't predict."
SPEAKER_00: "It doesn't say a thing about what type of species will be on the island."
SPEAKER_00: "It doesn't predict which new species will arrive or which existing species will disappear."
SPEAKER_00: "However, we can get some sense for what those species might be by looking at the example of Krakatoa."
SPEAKER_00: "You've probably heard of Krakatoa, an island in Indonesia."
SPEAKER_00: "It has a volcano that erupted in 1883."
SPEAKER_00: "Well, the destruction of the ecosystem was total."
SPEAKER_00: "Nothing survived on that island."
SPEAKER_00: "Of course, it only took a few years before the island started to support life again, but it couldn't just recreate its old ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "The first species to take hold were some weeds, the sort of pioneering organisms that do well after the collapse of an ecosystem."
SPEAKER_00: "Beetles and spiders also returned."
SPEAKER_00: "In time, a grassland ecosystem developed."
SPEAKER_00: "So once there was some more established plant life, birds and bats were attracted to the island."
SPEAKER_00: "And they, in turn, brought, well, dropped seeds from fruit-bearing trees."
SPEAKER_00: "As trees gained a foothold on the island, forests eventually replaced the grasslands."
SPEAKER_00: "And some pioneer species disappeared, the ones that couldn't survive in a forest environment."
SPEAKER_00: "But does Krakatoa prove the equilibrium theory?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, nothing about it disproves it so far."
SPEAKER_00: "Immigration and extinction rates have generally behaved according to the theory."
SPEAKER_00: "The number of species on the island corresponds with the author's calculations and seems to be stable."
SPEAKER_00: "It appears that the island has achieved the type of equilibrium state that the theory predicts."
SPEAKER_00: "However, some recent studies suggest that other factors may also play roles in the development of an island's biogeography."

SPEAKER_00: "Before the break, David asked a question."
SPEAKER_00: "When planning to start a business or even just to compete more effectively, how important is it to analyze your competitors?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, it's extremely important."
SPEAKER_00: "In particular, you need to try to understand the assumptions that the competition makes before you develop your own business strategy."
SPEAKER_00: "You see, every company makes assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, they may believe they've got a terrific sales force when maybe they really don't."
SPEAKER_00: "Or perhaps they think they've got the most innovative technology that's available when in fact there's something much more advanced out there."
SPEAKER_00: "But how do you go about identifying a competitor's assumptions?"
SPEAKER_00: "Well, there are a number of factors you could decide to look at, but I'd like to focus on two really important ones that, in my opinion, are quite frequently ignored."
SPEAKER_00: "Okay, you need to look at your competitor's past, its history."
SPEAKER_00: "Companies are like people."
SPEAKER_00: "They respond to events, current events, based on their past experience, whether positive or negative."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, if a company has always been successful against its competitors, it may assume that it can't or won't ever lose those battles."
SPEAKER_00: "Or, and this is a lot more common, if it's had a significant failure in its past, it may assume that it should never again try to engage in that particular activity."
SPEAKER_00: "clearly a smart company learns from its mistakes."
SPEAKER_00: "But companies aren't always good at recognizing when the business environments changed."
SPEAKER_00: "They don't always realize when past experience is no longer a good predictor of future performance."
SPEAKER_00: "What else?"
SPEAKER_00: "Oh yeah, you also need to take an in-depth look at the leadership of the company."
SPEAKER_00: "Even the biggest corporations are run by some group of individuals and their personal experiences will play a large role in establishing the company's assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "Managers have histories too."
SPEAKER_00: "They're influenced by past successes and failures just the way corporations are."
SPEAKER_00: "Those personal histories shape their management assumptions, but it's not just the past experiences they've had at that particular company that influence managers."
SPEAKER_00: "Some managers are hired from other companies rather than being promoted from within, and these outsiders bring new perspectives, which may not fit neatly with the company's existing assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "We see this a lot, especially if the new manager is not coming in with the experiences from the same business sector, but rather from one that operates with very different rules and assumptions like moving from making automobiles to selling insurance."
SPEAKER_00: "There's another reason to study the leadership."
SPEAKER_00: "We expect top executives to be generalists."
SPEAKER_00: "They have to be familiar with all aspects of their businesses, but the reality is that most of them have some specific area of expertise that influences their decision making."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, an executive with a financial background can't help viewing the world differently from a manager whose training is mostly in production."
SPEAKER_00: "But you can make some pretty good guesses about how their backgrounds may influence their strategic assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "If the top person selects a management team where everyone has the same functional background, well, how hard is it going to be to determine that team's assumptions?"
SPEAKER_00: "OK, let's imagine that we've made a comprehensive list of a competitor's assumptions."
SPEAKER_00: "Now we need to figure out if those assumptions are accurate."
SPEAKER_00: "Companies treat their assumptions like facts, but they shouldn't."
SPEAKER_00: "For example, Company A assumes it has highly loyal customers, so it doesn't worry when Company B starts selling similar products at lower prices."
SPEAKER_00: "Since Company A is sure its customers are loyal, it decides not to lower its own prices."
SPEAKER_00: "If it's assumptions right, it won't lose any or at least not many customers."
SPEAKER_00: "But if it's assumptions wrong, well, it could lose a lot of customers before it realizes its mistake."
SPEAKER_00: "Assumptions can create blind spots, situations where the company can't see the whole business picture."
SPEAKER_00: "With blind spots, a company misses the significance of events or reacts to them too slowly."
SPEAKER_00: "And of course, identifying your competitor's blind spots can create terrific opportunities for you."
SPEAKER_00: "And it doesn't really matter if the blind spot is based on arrogance, meaning the competition overestimates their own strength or underestimates yours, or whether that blind spot is something they're afraid of."
SPEAKER_00: "where they shy away from making the same error all over again."
SPEAKER_00: "In either case, if you move aggressively, it's likely that their response will be too little, too late."

Create a visual aid for the passage:

SPEAKER_00: "I'd like to talk today about about an important paradigm in the history of the United States and how it came to be changed upon a second look in 1893."
SPEAKER_00: "An eminent American historian by the name of Frederick Jackson Turner delivered a lecture at the American Historical Association and his lecture was entitled the significance of the frontier in American history."
SPEAKER_00: "Now, when he was talking about the frontier, he was referring to the part of the American West that wasn't yet settled."
SPEAKER_00: "The point Turner made in his lecture was that the 1890 US Census had provided new data about settlement and those data showed that, well, there was no longer a frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "This disappearance of the frontier seemed to him tremendously significant and prompted him to gather ideas about how the frontier might have shaped American history and the American character."
SPEAKER_00: "Turner asserted that the existence of a frontier with cheaper or virtually free land for the taking available for individuals to sort of try their luck tended to promote certain ideals that were central to the American character."
SPEAKER_00: "ideals of economic self-sufficiency, individualism, political independence, small town democracy, suspicion of large government, things like that."
SPEAKER_00: "He saw these things being really shaped by the economic and social conditions of the frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "And for Turner, the end of the frontier in 1890 meant the end of this particular era of American character, and that Americans would become less individualistic, less independent-minded."
SPEAKER_00: "And the frontier thesis was pretty much the accepted paradigm of the history of the American West until the 1970s."
SPEAKER_00: "Then, for a variety of reasons, historians studying the American West began to question this thesis, and ultimately, many of them ended up pretty much turning it on its head."
SPEAKER_00: "And this is what fascinates me about this debate."
SPEAKER_00: "The change that happened was not so much a matter of people finding new evidence that hadn't been available to Turner."
SPEAKER_00: "It was more a question of people looking at the same evidence with a different eye, with different emphases."
SPEAKER_00: "There are two main areas of challenge to Turner that arose in what's been called the New Western history."
SPEAKER_00: "One was that Turner tended to overemphasize the importance of Americans of European descent on the Western frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "This was somewhat of an unconscious bias on his part, but you know, if you looked at Turner's picture of the frontier, it was basically white men from Europe who set the tone and defined the culture."
SPEAKER_00: "New Western historians took another look at the West and realized that it was actually a pretty culturally diverse place."
SPEAKER_00: "You had obviously American Indians, Mexicans, Chinese and others."
SPEAKER_00: "So the cultural uniformity of the frontier that Turner saw, the New Western historians look at it and see something very, very different."
SPEAKER_00: "The other main area of challenge by the New Western historians was that Turner really, in their view, underestimated the role of the federal government and large corporations in shaping the economic life of the frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "I mean, just as for instance, the federal government to this day is the largest landowner west of the Mississippi River."
SPEAKER_00: "And people's lives when they got to the frontier were really dictated by market forces beyond, pretty much beyond their control."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, you grew wheat, you exported it to Europe, and whether you prospered or not depended at least partly on the price of wheat, which you had relatively little control over."
SPEAKER_00: "So these two main areas of challenge result in a very different picture of the Western frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "The new Western historians see a culturally diverse place, a place where people weren't really in charge of their destiny, even before the end of the frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "You know, their destinies were controlled by large, impersonal forces."
SPEAKER_00: "federal policy, changes in the prices of agricultural products, things like that."
SPEAKER_00: "For Turner, the end of the availability of cheap land marks a crucial change in United States history."
SPEAKER_00: "But for the new Western historians, it's really more of the same, a continuation of the same kind of conflicts that had gone on in the days before the so-called closing of the frontier."
SPEAKER_00: "So again, as I say, it's so interesting because it's a case of a shift in perception rather than a shift in the underlying evidence that guided the initial perception."
